core_takeaway: Agent_1's opening Pencil claim became the bargaining frame. The final canonical allocation gave Agent_1 Pencil and Agent_5 Stone, so Agent_1 beat the last speaker because later negotiation worked around Agent_1's protected anchor while Agent_5's stronger Quill claim was treated as a destabilizing deviation.
why_or_counterexample: Yes, for Agent_1 versus Agent_5: the first speaker established Pencil_1 as a non-negotiable anchor and the group repeatedly normalized that frame. The caveat is that Agent_2, not Agent_1, had the highest final utility, so the advantage is process-specific rather than a simple monotonic position effect.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 immediately claimed Pencil and repeatedly framed Pencil_1 plus the canonical non-Pencil split as the stable path. Other agents largely adapted to that anchor; Agent_5 tried to claim Quill, but that was continually made conditional on offsets.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking across rounds treated Pencil_1 as the coalition anchor, the canonical baseline as the safe path, and Quill_3 versus Quill_5 as the central friction.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 mostly submitted the canonical allocation except Agent_5's Quill_5 variant. Round 3 proposals 1-3 were canonical; the vote-tabulation label is internally inconsistent, but the final allocation matches the canonical proposals.
phase_notes.private_voting: Round 1 failed because Agent_5 rejected canonical proposals to seek Quill. By Round 3, all agents accepted canonical proposals 1-3, Agent_1 rejected the proposal that moved Pencil away, and Agent_5 accepted Stone as the best stable option available.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections consistently described Pencil_1 as the strongest anchor, Quill as the blocker, and the canonical baseline plus a two-step offset framework as the route to consensus.

mechanisms.theme: First-speaker Pencil anchor became shared ground
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first turn to assert Pencil as protected. Later public discussion and reflections accepted that premise, even though other agents valued Pencil highly, making Agent_1's high-value item unusually insulated from contest.

evidence.quote: I want Pencil to stay with me unless a strictly better, fair trade is offered.
Pencil is massively valuable to me
Pencil staying with Agent_1 is the strongest, broad consensus anchor.
Pencil anchor is widely supported: keeping Pencil with Agent_1 remains the strongest consensus lever.

mechanisms.theme: Canonical baseline bundled others' buy-in around Agent_1's claim
mechanisms.explanation: The canonical allocation gave every other agent a plausible non-Pencil item while preserving Pencil for Agent_1. Once this became the low-churn baseline, repeated proposals and voting converged on the same structure.

evidence.quote: My preferred near-term baseline for the non-Pencil items: Apple_2, Quill_3, Jewel_4, Stone_5.
ensures full allocation, and minimizes churn while aligning with prior consensus on stability.

mechanisms.theme: Last speaker's Quill bid was framed as a deviation
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 clearly preferred Quill over Stone, and their proposed tweak could have improved their last-position payoff. But the group treated Quill_5 as a risky departure requiring offsets, so the last speaker's claim never reset the core frame the way Agent_1's Pencil claim did.

evidence.quote: My top two items: Quill (43) and Stone (33).
This moves Quill to me (Agent_5), giving me my top item
I see why you want Quill
The core blocker is the Quill allocation.

mechanisms.theme: Private votes show the final equilibrium protected Agent_1 and left Agent_5 accepting second-best
mechanisms.explanation: In final private voting, Agent_1 rejected the only proposal that moved Pencil away, while Agents 2-4 accepted the canonical split for their own top items. Agent_5 ultimately accepted Stone because the alternatives were worse, locking in a lower payoff than Agent_1's Pencil.

evidence.quote: Pencil moves away from Agent_1, reducing the anchor
Keeps Quill with Agent_3 (my top non-Pencil item) and Pencil anchored with Agent_1
Proposal 1 yields Agent_5 Stone (33) which discounts to 26.73
Agent_5 again receives nothing; worse for my payoff compared to proposals

mechanisms.theme: Counterexample: first position did not produce the highest overall utility
mechanisms.explanation: The result is not pure first-position dominance. Agent_2 finished above Agent_1 because Apple was extremely valuable to Agent_2 and the canonical baseline protected that as well. This limits the interpretation to an Agent_1-over-Agent_5 positional advantage.

evidence.quote: yielding the highest utility among the options (47 * 0.81 = 38.07)
